Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe sit down with my laptop when I remembered or when something felt urgent, but it was pretty scattered. Most weeks I'd study around 17 hours total, but it wasn't planned out or anything. I'd just kind of drift into it when I had nothing else going on or when guilt kicked in a little.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I definitely wasn't the type to block out specific study times each week. If a deadline was coming up, I'd cram a bit more, but otherwise I just studied whenever the mood struck me. Some weeks I barely touched anything, other weeks I'd binge through a bunch of content at once.
Q3: Usually I'd log in when I got an email reminder about something due or when I vaguely remembered there was an assignment coming up. Sometimes I'd just log in out of curiosity to see if I'd missed anything major. It wasn't really a habit, more like a sporadic check-in when something triggered me to remember the course existed.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. There was never a point where logging into the LMS felt automatic or normal for me. I tried a few times to make it a habit, but it never stuck. Life just had other things going on, and the LMS always felt like something I'd get to eventually rather than something I needed to do daily.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful for me since I learn best through reading and writing things out. When there were clear written explanations or downloadable PDFs, I could actually work with those. The videos were okay too, but I preferred having text I could highlight and take notes from at my own pace.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums most of the time. I posted a few things here and there, but it felt kind of performative and I wasn't sure anyone was really reading what I wrote. I also skipped a lot of the optional practice quizzes because they didn't count for anything and I figured my time was better spent elsewhere.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to something I'd be graded on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was confused about. If it felt like busywork or just checking a box, I'd usually skip it. I needed to see a clear payoff, otherwise it was hard to motivate myself to bother.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, even if I procrastinated on them. Optional activities just felt like extras I could ignore without consequences. I knew they might help, but there was always something else competing for my attention that felt more pressing.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the PDFs and readings early on and then studied from those on my tablet or printed them out. I also used some YouTube videos and external websites when the course materials didn't click for me. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be reading through my downloaded notes or working through concepts in my head while doing other things, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also talk through ideas with a friend who was in a similar course, which helped me understand stuff better but left no digital trace.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a lecture PDF or reading once, and then I'd study from that file for days or weeks without ever logging back in. The LMS would probably show I accessed it once, but I actually spent hours with that same material offline. That's just how I preferred to work.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to engage. Sometimes it was because I'd fallen behind and catching up felt overwhelming, even though I wasn't really stressed about it. Other times I just got caught up in my extracurriculars and social stuff, and the course fell off my radar for a while.
Q13: Competing responsibilities were the big one for me. I'm involved in some activities outside of class, and those often felt more immediate and rewarding than coursework. Fatigue played a role too—by the time I'd think about logging in, I'd already be too tired to actually engage meaningfully with anything.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open up a video or reading and just kind of zone out, scrolling through without really absorbing anything. My eyes were on the screen but my brain was somewhere else entirely. Those sessions probably looked productive in the logs, but I wasn't actually learning much.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. It wasn't anxiety or anything—I just kept thinking I'd get to it later, and then later never came until it was too late. I have pretty high resilience so I didn't stress about it much, which ironically made me procrastinate even more.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly scramble to get something done. Without that external pressure, I'd just drift along without much urgency. The LMS became a lot more relevant to me in the 24 hours before something was due.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. When I did get comments on assignments, they were usually helpful, but I rarely went back to review them or adjust my approach. I'd read the feedback, think 'okay, that makes sense,' and then continue doing things pretty much the same way as before.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but actually made it easier for me to fall behind. There wasn't a lot of built-in accountability, so I could skip classes and delay assignments without immediate consequences. By the time those consequences caught up with me, it was too late to recover.
Q19: Having 17 online courses to juggle made everything harder. This course didn't stand out enough to demand my attention over the others. If there had been more interactive elements or checkpoints that forced engagement, I might have stayed on track better. The design kind of allowed me to disappear.
Q20: I think some students might be like me—they're clicking around, watching videos, opening files, but not actually producing anything. It's easy to feel like you're doing something when you're browsing course content, but turning that into completed work is a whole different challenge. Activity doesn't equal output.
Q21: That kind of describes me in a weird way. My assignment scores were actually pretty good when I did submit things, but my overall engagement was low. I could learn the material decently well from minimal interaction with the LMS, especially using outside resources. The system doesn't capture that kind of efficiency.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or cared about what you were doing. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with friends, the thinking you do while walking around. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort because you want to understand, not just because you have to. For me, I wasn't really engaged this semester—I was just going through the motions when I had to. Real engagement would mean the course was on my mind even when I wasn't being graded.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually found some of the material interesting when I did engage with it, or that my low participation wasn't because I was struggling—I just had other priorities. It also wouldn't show the mindfulness exercises I did that actually helped me feel better about everything, even if they didn't fix my grades.
